coredata.dc:title: Valorisation of bio-derived fluorescent carbon dots for metal sensing, DNA binding and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Jacinth Gracia, Kirubaharan Daphne
Thavamani, Seth Sheeba
Amaladhas, Thomas Peter
Devanesan, Sandhanasamy
Ahmed, Mukhtar
Kannan, Maruthamuthu Murali
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots are quasi-spherical and zero dimensional nanomaterials with unique optical and electronic properties. In this work, a facile and sustainable strategy was employed to synthesise nitrogen doped carbon dots from Terminalia chebula via hydrothermal treatment with a quantum yield of 19.9%. The structural and optical properties of nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were studied by UV–Visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The surface functional groups, average particle size and elemental analysis were assessed with the help of Fourier Transform Infra Red spectroscopy, High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy and Energy Dispersive X-ray analysis respectively. The N-CDs exhibited excitation dependent emission upon irradiation with UV light, pH stability over neutral range and excellent photostability. The average particle size of the synthesised N-CDs was found to be 3.56 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs quenched linearly with increase in concentration of Fe3+ ions. The limit of detection (LOD) of N-CDs with Fe3+ ions was calculated to be 4.5 nM using Stern-Volmer plot. The fluorescence was restored by addition of EDTA to Fe3+ coordinated N-CD system. Further, the synthesised N-CDs interacted with ct-DNA through intercalative mode and the binding constant calculated using the Benesi Hildebrand plot was 1.78 × 108 mg/mL. The cytotoxicity of N-CDs was evaluated using MTT assay. The excellent biocompatible and less toxic nature of N-CDs was extrapolated to serve as fluorescent probes for imaging E.coli and SKMEL cells. From the results of this work, it is evident that the synthesised N-CDs can be used to develop efficient fluorescent metal sensors. The fluorescent property of N-CDs enables it to find extension as a potential curative drug, an efficient patterning agent and an effective biomarker to image biological cells causing no damage to normal cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Terminalia chebula
Photoluminescence
Fe3+ detection
ct-DNA binding
Bio-imaging

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Pharmaceutical and personal care products (PPCPs) treatment and sensing by 2D carbon nanomaterials; challenges and perspectives, edited by Hassan Karimi-Maleh, Yasin Orooji and Mehmet Lutfi Yola

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge IIT Bombay SAIF, for recording HR-TEM and The Gandhigram Rural Institute - Deemed University for recording EDX. The authors record their gratitude to DST-FIST for funding Department of Chemistry, V.O. Chidambaram College. The authors are grateful to the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP-2021/398), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis of N-CDs.
(a) Absorption spectrum of N-CDs and (b) Fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs at various excitation wavelengths.
HR-TEM analysis of N-CDs (a–b) HR-TEM images, (c) particle size distribution and (d) SAED pattern.
Effect of (a) pH and (b) time on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs.

(a) A bar plot of the emission intensity N-CDs Vs various metal ions at 0.01 M concentration, (b) Fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs with Fe3+ ions in the concentration range 8 × 10−8 M to 4 × 10−5 M, (c) The fluorescence exhibited by the synthesised N-CDs in the presence of different metal ions and (d) The Stern-Volmer plot of F0–F Vs the concentration of Fe3+ ions.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of NCD-Fe3+ complex upon addition of EDTA in the range 1 μM–100 μM at excitation wavelength 350 nm, (b) A plot of fluorescence intensity against the concentration of EDTA, (c) Turn-off-on fluorescent sensor with N-CDs and (d) Patterning image drawn with N-CDs as fluorescent ink under UV light.
(a) UV absorption spectra of ct-DNA with various concentrations of N-CDs and (b) Benesi-Hildebrand plot for the estimation of binding constant.
(a) Blank SKMEL cells, (b) SKMEL cells with N-CDs at 100 μL concentration, (c) Blank L6 cells and (d) L6 cells with N-CDs at 100 μL concentration.
Cytotoxicity evaluation of nitrogen doped carbon dots by MTT assay.

Fluorescence microscope images (a) SKMEL cells in the absence of N-CDs, (b) SKMEL cells in the presence of N-CDs, (c) E.coli cells in the absence of N-CDs and (d) E.coli cells in the presence of N-CDs.
Comparison of the limit of detection of Fe3+ ions by carbon dots.

row.entry: Limit of Detection of Fe3+ ions

head.ce:title: Valorisation of bio-derived fluorescent carbon dots for metal sensing, DNA binding and bioimaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots are quasi-spherical and zero dimensional nanomaterials with unique optical and electronic properties. In this work, a facile and sustainable strategy was employed to synthesise nitrogen doped carbon dots from Terminalia chebula via hydrothermal treatment with a quantum yield of 19.9%. The structural and optical properties of nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were studied by UV–Visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The surface functional groups, average particle size and elemental analysis were assessed with the help of Fourier Transform Infra Red spectroscopy, High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy and Energy Dispersive X-ray analysis respectively. The N-CDs exhibited excitation dependent emission upon irradiation with UV light, pH stability over neutral range and excellent photostability. The average particle size of the synthesised N-CDs was found to be 3.56 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs quenched linearly with increase in concentration of Fe3+ ions. The limit of detection (LOD) of N-CDs with Fe3+ ions was calculated to be 4.5 nM using Stern-Volmer plot. The fluorescence was restored by addition of EDTA to Fe3+ coordinated N-CD system. Further, the synthesised N-CDs interacted with ct-DNA through intercalative mode and the binding constant calculated using the Benesi Hildebrand plot was 1.78 × 108 mg/mL. The cytotoxicity of N-CDs was evaluated using MTT assay. The excellent biocompatible and less toxic nature of N-CDs was extrapolated to serve as fluorescent probes for imaging E.coli and SKMEL cells. From the results of this work, it is evident that the synthesised N-CDs can be used to develop efficient fluorescent metal sensors. The fluorescent property of N-CDs enables it to find extension as a potential curative drug, an efficient patterning agent and an effective biomarker to image biological cells causing no damage to normal cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials, in general, have unique optical, electronic, magnetic and chemical properties when compared to their bulk counterparts owing to their large surface to volume ratio (Edison et al., 2016). Ever since the discovery of fluorescent carbon dots, a new class of carbon nanomaterials of size less than 10 nm, it has attracted huge research interest due to the less toxic nature, excellent biocompatibility, stability in aqueous solutions, tunable optical and electronic properties, excitation dependent emission, easy and low cost of synthesis. Carbon dots are composed of multi-crystalline or amorphous carbon core with a considerable amount of sp2 clusters (Mintz et al., 2021; Yao et al., 2019). Synthesis of carbon dots from carbonaceous materials are carried out by various techniques including electric arc discharge method, laser ablation, chemical oxidation, thermal decomposition, electrochemical, microwave and hydrothermal methods. Of all the techniques available to synthesise carbon dots, hydrothermal method has a niche as it is environment friendly, affordable and involves simple synthetic procedures (Das et al., 2018). Heteroatom doping results in the fine tuning of the surface functionalities and the structure of electronic energy levels (Batool et al., 2020). Also, doping results in the enhancement of the quantum yield and emission intensity of the carbon dots. Of all the dopants, nitrogen being comparable in size to that of carbon and with five valence electrons is considered as an ideal dopant as it serves as n-type impurity providing excess electrons and thereby resulting in a shift in the Fermi levels and enhanced optical and electronic properties (Yao et al., 2019). Ammonia, ethylenediamine, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid and urea are the commonly used nitrogen dopants (Mintz et al., 2019). Doping enhances the overall efficiency of the carbon dots and contributes to the enhanced functioning of carbon dots as biomarkers, metal sensors and catalysts in the degradation of toxic environmental pollutants (Liu et al., 2019).

Terminalia chebula Retz. (chebulic myrobalan) belonging to the family Combretaceae is a native plant of India and Southeast Asia. The chemical constituents of Terminalia chebula, viz. hydrolysable tannins, flavanols, glycosides, triterpenoids, vitamin C, amino acids and polyphenolic compounds, make it a suitable carbon precursor for the synthesis of carbon dots (Li et al., 2019). Carbonisation, nitrogen doping and surface functionalization occur simultaneously during hydrothermal treatment, and the lone pairs of electrons in ethylenediamine coordinate better with the surface functionalities and hence it is considered to be an ideal nitrogen dopant (Liu et al., 2019). The heteroatom rich constituents of Terminalia chebula enrich the surface of the N-CDs with abundant functional groups that are responsible for the excellent hydrophilicity and biocompatibility, enabling it to be a potent system in the field of sensors and bioimaging.

Fluorescent detection methods have drawn significant momentum as it requires no sophisticated instrumentation and laborious procedures, besides having the advantages of high sensitivity, operational simplicity, excellent selectivity, low cost and short response time (Shanmugaraj and John, 2018; Huang et al., 2018). Ferric ion (Fe3+) is one of the indispensable metal ions that plays a prominent role in several physiological processes such as metabolism at cellular levels, transport of oxygen and in the synthesis of RNA (Chan et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018). Deficiency of Fe3+ leads to neurological function disorder, anaemia, diabetes, heart failure, and hypoimmunity. Early diagnosis of ferric ion deficiency is crucial for monitoring and control of such diseases.

The interaction of DNA with molecules is a crucial component of research in the field of medicinal chemistry. Carbon dots bind to the double-helical structure of the DNA via three major modes namely electrostatic interaction, groove binding and intercalation. DNA is a carrier of genetic information and is a target for various drugs in vivo. The binding interaction between calf thymus DNA (ct-DNA) and the synthesised N-CDs has been studied and found to be a potential and effective substitute to organic fluorescent dyes as the latter post high environmental hazards owing to its high toxicity and poor biocompatibility (Liang et al., 2018). Contrary to conventional semiconductor quantum dots, carbon dots have no cytotoxic effects and they exhibit excellent biocompatibility with biological cells and hence its applications can be expanded to image cells as an extension of its fluorescent property.

Herein, we report a simple efficient and environment friendly method to synthesise nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) from Terminalia chebula via hydrothermal treatment. The synthesised N-CDs are characterised by UV–Visible, fluorescence spectroscopy, High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM), Energy Dispersive X-ray Analysis (EDX) and Fourier Transform Infra Red (FTIR) spectroscopy. An efficient selective and sensitive fluorescent turn-off-on sensor to detect Fe3+ ions and EDTA has been developed. Further, the interaction between N-CDs and ct-DNA and the cytotoxic effects of N-CDs towards SKMEL and L6 cells has also been investigated along with its ability to serve as fluorescent biomarkers.

CoCl2.6H2O, NiCl2.6H2O, CuSO4.6H2O (SRL, 99.0%), anhydrous ZnCl2 (Alfa Aesar, 98.0%), anhydrous CrCl3 (99.9%), anhydrous FeCl3 (99.9%), (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2.6H2O (99.9%), HgCl2 (99.5%), Pb(NO3)2 (99.0%) and Cd(NO3)2.4H2O (98.0%) (Sigma Aldrich) were procured and used without further purification for metal sensing studies. Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid sodium salt dihydrate (99.0%) used for turn-on fluorescent sensor studies was procured from Sigma Aldrich and used as received. Nitrogen dopant ethylenediamine was purchased from (SRL, 99.5%). Quinine sulphate (99.0%) used for the quantum yield measurement and ct-DNA were procured from Sigma Aldrich. Emparta grade sodium chloride (99.0%) and Tris HCl (99.0%) were obtained from Merck. Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium was purchased from Himedia. All other chemicals used were of AR grade and used as received. Double distilled water was used throughout the course of the work.

A drop of N-CDs placed on the copper grid was allowed to dry at room temperature and HR-TEM analysis was carried out to investigate the morphology and size of N-CDs using Tecnai G2, F30 at 200 kV acceleration voltage. Energy Dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) to study the constituent elements of the synthesised N-CDs was done with the help of Bruker Nano, GmbH, D-12489 with an accelerating voltage of 30 kV. Zeta potential analysis was performed to assess the surface charge of the synthesised N-CDs before and after complexation using Zetasizer 1000 HS (Malvern Instruments, UK) at Defence Food Research Laboratory, Mysore. Shimadzu FT-IR-8400S spectrophotometer was used to record the ATR-FTIR spectrum of N-CDs in the range 400–4000 cm−1. The optical and electronic properties of the synthesised N-CDs were investigated using JASCO V-650 spectrophotometer and JASCO FP-8300 spectrofluorometer. Cytotoxicity studies were carried out using a Labomed TCM-400 tissue culture microscope.

Terminalia chebula was bought from the nearby market in Tuticorin, pulverized to a fine powder and stored in an airtight container for the synthesis of N-CDs. For the synthesis of N-CDs, 0.4 g of Terminalia chebula powder along with distilled water (30 mL) and 0.5 mL of ethylenediamine was taken in a 50 mL stainless steel autoclave with Teflon lining and hydrothermal treatment was carried out at 180 °C for 6 h (Xu et al., 2015). The autoclave was then naturally cooled down to room temperature and the resultant brown solution was filtered using Whatman filter paper (11 μm pore size) to remove large particles. The light brown filtrate was further purified with a cellulose nitrate membrane filter of pore size 0.22 μm and stored at the refrigerator (4 °C) for further characterisation and use.

For calculating the quantum yield, quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 was used as a reference standard. Quinine sulphate's quantum yield at 350 nm was reported to be 0.54 (n = 1.33). To minimise the reabsorption effects, at excitation wavelength 350 nm the absorbances were kept under 0.1. The quantum yield was calculated using Eq. (1) (Atchudan et al., 2018a):
(1)
Φ =
Φ R
×
m x
/
m
s t
×
n x 2
/
n
s t
2
where, the subscripts x and st represent N-CD sample and standard respectively, ɸ, m and n represent the quantum yield, gradient of the plot of luminescence intensity Vs absorbance and refractive index respectively.

The emission intensity of the synthesised N-CDs in the presence of different metal ions such as Hg2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Cd2+ and Ni2+ at 0.01 M concentration was investigated to evaluate the ability of the synthesised N-CDs to sense metals. The stock solutions of different metal ions were prepared using the respective metal salt precursors. Twenty μL of diluted N-CDs was added to 0.2 mL of Tris HCl buffer solution, and then different metal ions were added to the total volume of 1 mL. The solutions were shaken well and kept at room temperature for 3 min and the spectral measurements were done. All spectral measurements were recorded at λ ex = 350 nm and at pH 7, corresponding to the emission maxima of the synthesised N-CDs.

For restoration of the already quenched fluorescence (Turn-on fluorescent sensor) of the synthesised N-CDs, 4 mL diluted N-CDs along with Tris HCl buffer (0.5 mL), 0.5 mL of 500 μM Fe3+ and 1 mL EDTA were shaken well and kept at room temperature for 3 min and the spectral measurements were done. All the spectra were recorded at 350 nm excitation wavelength.

The efficient binding of the synthesised N-CDs with ct-DNA was investigated with the help of UV–Visible spectrophotometer. For the preparation DNA solution, 0.01 g of ct-DNA in 10 mL of 5 mM Tris HCl and 50 mM NaCl was sonicated for 20 cycles of 1 min with an interval of 20 s each for homogeneity. The absorbance ratio at 260 nm and 280 nm (A260/A280) was 2, which indicated the absence of contaminants in the prepared DNA solution (Jhonsi et al., 2018). The stock solution was stored at 4 °C and used within 4 days. The concentration of ct-DNA (50 μL) was fixed and the UV–Visible absorption spectra were recorded for the increasing concentration of N-CDs (120 μL–900 μL) and a pH of 7.4 was maintained using Tris HCl. A total volume of 1 mL was maintained throughout the experiment. The absorption spectra were recorded after a uniform incubation time of 30 s.

The cytotoxic effect of the synthesised N-CDs was studied on both SKMEL cell line (Human Skin cancer) and L6 cell line (Rat skeletal muscle) at various concentrations. The N-CDs were prepared in DMEM media (100 mg/mL). The samples were further diluted in DMEM media and seeded to the wells containing cultured cells at final concentrations of 6.25 μg–100 μg respectively. Untreated wells were kept as control. To minimise errors, all experiments were performed in triplicate and the average values were taken. After treatment with the test samples, the plates were further incubated for 24 h. A microplate reader was used to measure absorbance at 570 nm.

Sample fluorescence was detected with SKMEL and E. coli cell lines. The cells cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) were supplemented with 10% heat inactivated Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) and 1% antibiotic cocktail containing Penicillin (100 U/mL), Streptomycin (100 μg/mL), and Amphotericin B (2.5 μg/mL). N-CDs were incubated with the cells for 1 h and were further analysed by fluorescence microscopy.

Fig. 1 represents the schematic representation of the synthesis of N-CDs. The optical and electronic properties of the synthesised N-CDs were examined with the help of UV–Visible spectroscopy. Fig. 2 a, represents the UV–Visible absorption spectrum of the synthesised N-CDs. Peaks at 257 nm and 281 nm correspond to π-π* electronic transition and the hump at 335 nm is due to n-π* electronic transition. The conjugated aromatic π domains are responsible for π-π* electronic transition and heteroatom functional groups are responsible for n-π* electronic transition (Krishnaiah et al., 2022; Kumar et al., 2017). The synthesised N-CDs are observed to exhibit excitation dependent emission as depicted in Fig. 2b. The excitation dependent emission of the synthesised N-CDs is governed by the relative population in the emissive states. The different particle sizes and the various surface emissive states due to the different surface functional groups are responsible for the excitation dependent emission of the N-CDs. The emission intensity of the synthesised N-CDs is found to vary with the variation of excitation wavelength. From the emission spectra, it is observed that the maximum λ ex and λ em correspond to 350 nm and 430 nm respectively. The decrease in energy levels on nitrogen doping causes a red shift in the emission wavelength of the synthesised N-CDs. The presence of various surface emissive sites with functional groups are also responsible for the shift in the emission wavelength. Radiative recombination of the excited non-bonding electrons of the carbonyl functional groups and nitrogen present in the synthesised N-CDs, the electronegativity of the atoms in the functional groups and quantum confinement effect are the reasons held responsible for the origin of fluorescence in the synthesised N-CDs (Krishnaiah et al., 2022). The quantum yield of the synthesised N-CDs is calculated to be 19.9% with quinine sulphate as the reference. The quantum yield calculations are presented in S 1. The quantum yield of the carbon dots synthesised from Terminalia chebula without nitrogen dopant is 7.2%. Thus, the quantum yields and efficiency of the carbon dots enhance as a result of nitrogen doping, a similar trend is reported elsewhere (Edison et al., 2016).

The FT-IR spectrum of the synthesised N-CDs is presented in the supplementary data (S 2). The surface functional groups were analysed from the FT-IR spectrum. Peaks at 679 cm−1, 647 cm−1 and 576 cm−1 correspond to the presence of –CH2 groups in the synthesised N-CDs (Boobalan et al., 2020). Peaks at 3446 cm−1, 1640 cm−1, 1509 cm−1 and 2882 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of –OH groups, carbonyl (CO) groups, stretching vibration of aromatic C–N heterocycles and symmetric stretching vibration of C–H respectively (Kumar et al., 2017; Boobalan et al., 2020). Peaks at 1074 cm−1, 1170 cm−1 and 2306 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of ether linkages, stretching vibration of C–N and intramolecular H-bridges in N-CDs respectively (Atchudan et al., 2018a; Arul et al., 2017). The excellent hydrophilic nature of the synthesised N-CDs roots to the presence of abundant heteroatom containing functional groups on the surface (Das et al., 2018; Wei et al., 2019).

The surface morphology and size of the synthesised N-CDs was studied using HR-TEM imaging as represented in Fig. 3 . The synthesised N-CDs appear to be quasi-spherical, with obvious lattice fringes, as seen in Fig. 3a. The diameter of the N-CDs ranges between 2 nm and 6 nm with an average particle size of 3.56 nm. The crystalline nature of synthesised N-CDs is indicated by clear lattice fringes. The d spacing value is calculated to be approximately 0.28 nm, which is due to the presence of the (020) plane corresponding to the graphitic carbon (sp2) (Su et al., 2020). Also, the diffused nature of the ring patterns in the Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) pattern of the synthesised N-CDs, indicates the amorphous nature of carbon (Sachdev and Gopinath, 2015).

Energy Dispersive X-ray Analysis (EDX) technique was used to confirm the constituent elements of the synthesised N-CDs as well as the extent of nitrogen doping. S 3 depicts the EDX from Scanning Electron Microscopy analysis. It is evident from the spectrum that the synthesised N-CDs are constituted mainly of carbon (43.20%), nitrogen (23.71%) and oxygen (33.08%). The abundant oxygen rich carbonyl and hydroxyl functional groups present make up for the highest percentage of carbon and oxygen in the synthesised N-CDs.

The chemical structure and crystalline nature of the synthesised N-CDs are characterised with the help of Raman spectroscopy as represented in S 4. Bands at 1442 cm−1 and 1676 cm−1 correspond to D band and G band respectively. The intense G band is the Raman active mode of graphite that contributes to the sp2 hybridisation of the synthesised N-CDs. D band indicates the disordered sp3 hybridised carbon. The intensity ratio of the D band and G band (ID/IG) is calculated to be 0.8, indicating the moderate degree of graphitisation (Atchudan et al., 2018b; Thambiraj and Shankaran, 2016). The one at 1639 cm−1 corresponds to D′ band. The relatively broad peaks at 1491 cm−1, 1557 cm−1 and 1614 cm−1 correspond to the amorphous carbon present on the surface of the synthesised N-CDs (Shimodaira and Masui, 2002). This further validates the amorphous nature of the synthesised N-CDs confirmed from HR-TEM.

Fig. 4 a represents the effect of pH on the emission intensity of the synthesised N-CDs. The emission intensity of the synthesised N-CDs is observed to be pH sensitive with maximum intensity over the neutral pH (Liang et al., 2020). The protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups are the reasons for the change in emission intensity at both acidic and basic conditions. The protonation of functional groups results in intramolecular hydrogen bonding. This leads to the aggregation of N-CDs accompanied by the loss of fluorescence intensity. The negative charges from the deprotonated functional groups result in the formation of an anionic layer on the surface of the synthesised N-CDs. This disrupts the origin of fluorescence thereby decreasing its emission intensity (Kaur et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2015). The maximum and stable emission intensity over the neutral pH range makes the synthesised N-CDs potential probes for imaging and other biological applications.

On continuous irradiation with Xenon lamp for 60 min, the emission intensity of the synthesised N-CDs is found to remain constant with no evident change as depicted in Fig. 4b. The synthesised N-CDs exhibited excellent stability and resistance to photobleaching for a period of six months without any decrease in the emission intensity when stored at 4 °C.

The negatively charged surface of the synthesised N-CDs offers ambient conditions for complex formation with metal cations, which in turn disrupts the fluorescence origin. This can be extended to study the ability of the synthesised N-CDs to sense metals with great sensitivity. From Fig. 5 a, it is evident that the ferric ions quench the emission intensity of the synthesised N-CDs to a greater extent of about 99%. Hence, the synthesised N-CDs can serve as an efficient selective and sensitive fluorescent probe for the detection of ferric ions. With an increase in the concentration of the ferric ions in the range 8 × 10−8 M to 4 × 10−5 M, a decrease in the emission intensity of the synthesised N-CDs is observed as shown in Fig. 5b. The intensity of the N-CDs in the presence of ferric ions is tabulated in S 5. The concentration of the ferric ions and the emission intensity of the synthesised N-CDs are in a linear relationship in the range 8 × 10−8 M to 8 × 10−7 M (R2 = 0.99) as depicted in Fig. 5d. The detection limit is calculated from Eq. (2)
(2)
L O D =
3 σ
s l o p e
where, σ is the standard deviation of blank (n = 10) (Das et al., 2018).

The detection limit is calculated to be 4.5 nM and the limit of quantification is calculated from Eq. (3) (Shrivastava and Gupta, 2011) and is found to be 15 nM.
(3)
L O Q =
10 σ
s l o p e

The strong affinity of ferric ions towards the surface phenolic hydroxyl/amine groups results in the formation of a complex that leads to the quenching of fluorescence. During this process, excited electrons of N-CDs are transferred to the Fe3+ half-filled 3d orbitals, causing a non-radiative electron/hole recombination that result in the significant quenching of fluorescence (Zulfajri et al., 2019).

A comparison of the limit of detection of Fe3+ ions by carbon dots derived from various sources as reported in different literature is presented in Table 1 . The detection limit of 4.5 nM achieved in this study is the lowest among all.

The NCD-Fe3+ complex formed was examined with the help of the FT-IR spectroscopy technique. The FT-IR spectral characteristics of the synthesised N-CDs on the addition of Fe3+ ions is represented in S 6. The shift in the peaks suggests the formation of the NCD-Fe3+ complex. The shift in peaks is the result of the structural changes that arise from the interaction of Fe3+ ions with the synthesised N-CDs. An evident decrease in the intensity of the peak at 3444 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 confirms the interaction between the surface hydroxyl and carboxyl group and Fe3+ ions. Also the shift in the peaks at 1436 cm−1 corresponding to –OH bending (Mandal and Das, 2020), 3048 cm−1 corresponding to N–H stretching, 1176 cm−1 corresponding to C–N stretching and the peaks at 679 cm−1, 647 cm−1 and 576 cm−1 corresponding to the –CH2 groups in the synthesised N-CDs (Boobalan et al., 2020) indicates that the NCD-Fe3+ complex changes the electronic structure of the N-CDs, resulting in a difference in the distribution of excitons which leads to fluorescence quenching of the synthesised N-CDs as a result of non-radiative recombination (Han et al., 2016).

The change in the surface charge of the N-CDs and the NCD-Fe3+ complex from - 6 mV to 0 mV further confirms complex formation between N-CDs and Fe3+ ions. The heteroatom rich functional groups contribute to the negative surface of the N-CDs and the coordination of ferric ions to the surface functional groups result in the neutralisation of the surface negative charges.

The fluorescent characteristic of the Fe3+ coordinated N-CD system was studied in the presence of EDTA. On addition of EDTA to NCD-Fe3+ complex, the fluorescence is restored back to the system as shown in Fig. 6 . Fluorescence enhancement is the result of the stronger binding affinity of EDTA towards Fe3+ ions. This is the driving force to expel ferric ions out of the already formed NCD-Fe3+ complex, resulting in the fluorescence restoration (Han et al., 2016) (S 7).

Fig. 6d, depicts the potential aspect of the synthesised N-CDs to serve as fluorescent ink which can also be extended as a patterning agent. Conveying confidential messages becomes much easier with such fluorescent probes that can function as patterning agents. For this purpose, N-CDs were used to write on a commercially available paper, air dried and then imaged under UV light. Highly fluorescent images obtained make the synthesised N-CDs an efficient patterning agent (Bhatt et al., 2018).

The interaction of N-CDs with ct-DNA results in hyperchromic or hypochromic effects due to the change in the double-helical structure of the DNA. The characteristic DNA peak at 260 nm corresponds to the π-π* transition of the sugar phosphates (Velammal and Amaladhas, 2021). Generally, the breakage of the secondary structure of DNA results in hyperchromism, accompanied by a decrease in the absorbance of ct-DNA. The stabilisation of DNA by intercalative binding mode is accompanied by an increase in the absorbance of the DNA (Liang et al., 2018; Boobalan et al., 2020). π-π stacking and dipole-dipole interactions stabilise the intercalative binding between ct-DNA and N-CDs (Jhonsi et al., 2018).

The absorbance increases as the concentration of the synthesised N-CDs increases along with a slight red shift as shown in Fig. 7 . This indicates intercalative interaction between the synthesised N-CDs and ct-DNA (Velammal et al., 2016, 2021). The binding constant for the interaction between ct-DNA and N-CDs was calculated using Benesi-Hildebrand equation (4) and it is found to be 1.78 × 108 mg/mL [equivalent to 5.6 × 10−8 mg/mL)−1 (S 8).
(4)
1
A
o b s
−
A 0
=
1
A C
−
A O
+
1
K
(
A
C
−
A O
)
[
N − C D s
]

The presence of heteroatom rich surface functional groups favour strong H-bonding between N-CDs and ct-DNA base pairs (Pramanik et al., 2018). The limit of detection calculated from the formula as in Eq. (5) (Liang et al., 2020) is found to be 19.45 mg/mL.
(5)
L O D =
3 σ
s l o p e
where, σ is the standard deviation of intercept.

The limit of quantification calculated from the formula as in Eq. (6) is found to be 41.7 mg/mL.
(6)
L O Q = N
x
σ
s l o p e
where, N is the number of readings and σ is the standard.

N-CDs are potential probes for imaging cells owing to their biocompatibility and excellent resistance towards photo degradation. From Fig. 8 , it is evident that the nitrogen doped carbon dots do not exhibit any significant toxicity towards normal cells (L6 cell line) at all the concentrations chosen for study. But a decrease in cell viability with respect to dose is observed in the case of cancer cells (SKMEL cell line) on the administration of different concentrations of nitrogen doped carbon dots. The L6 cells retain the viability up to 94.5% as shown in Fig. 9 and it is found to be comparable with the existing reports (Jiliang et al., 2017). Thus, the N-CDs can be further employed for biological applications.

The ability of the N-CDs to image E.coli cells and SKMEL cells were exploited as shown in Fig. 10 . Multicolour emission at different wavelengths is a unique property of carbon dots. The N-CDs (40 μL) treated cells exhibit green fluorescence when excited at 460 nm (Arul et al., 2017). The well dispersed fluorescent images of the cells indicate the successful penetration of the synthesised N-CDs within the cell membrane (Velammal and Amaladhas, 2021). From the non-degradable nature of the cells even after internalisation of the synthesised N-CDs, it is evident that the N-CDs possess excellent biocompatibility and wavelength-specific emission within the bacterial cells (Kasibabu et al., 2015).

In summary, we have reported a low cost, facile hydrothermal method to synthesise fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots from Terminalia chebula as a carbon precursor and ethylene diamine as the nitrogen dopant. The presence of nitrogen and other surface functional groups, the size and morphology of the synthesised N-CDs were confirmed with FT-IR, EDAX and HR-TEM respectively. The N-CDs exhibited excitation dependent emission with a quantum yield of 19.9% in the aqueous medium. The N-CDs exhibited multicolour emissions (blue and green) when excited at different wavelengths. The synthesised N-CDs can be used as an efficient probe to selectively sense Fe3+ ions with a detection limit of 4.5 nM. The quenched fluorescence was restored back with EDTA and thus the synthesised N-CDs serve as a dual turn-on-off fluorescent sensor. The synthesised N-CDs have been found to exhibit intercalative interaction with ct-DNA with a binding constant of 1.78 × 108 mg/mL. Besides, N-CDs showed less cytotoxic effect towards L6 cells and significant toxicity towards SKMEL cells. The potential properties of N-CDs can further be employed in the in-vivo imaging of cell lines and as patterning agents.

Kirubaharan Daphne Jacinth Gracia: Data curation; Formal analysis; Investigation; Methodology; Project administration; Resources; Software; Writing - original draft. Seth Sheeba Thavamani: Conceptualization; Project administration; Supervision; Validation; Writing - review & editing. Thomas Peter Amaladhas: Conceptualization; Data curation;; Funding acquisition; Investigation; Methodology; Project administration; Resources; Supervision; Validation; Visualization; Writing - review & editing. Mukhtar Ahmed: Funding acquisition; Data curation; Investigation; Methodology. Sandhanasamy Devanesan: Funding acquisition; Data curation; Investigation; Methodology. Maruthamuthu Murali Kannan: Funding acquisition; Data curation; Investigation; Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge IIT Bombay SAIF, for recording HR-TEM and The Gandhigram Rural Institute - Deemed University for recording EDX. The authors record their gratitude to DST-FIST for funding Department of Chemistry, V.O. Chidambaram College. The authors are grateful to the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP-2021/398), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

Calculation of quantum yield of N-CDs; FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs; EDX spectrum of N-CDs; Raman spectrum of NCDs; Optimisation of N-CDs as “turn-off-on” fluorescent sensor with Fe3+ & EDTA; FT-IR spectrum of NCD-Fe3+ complex, ct-DNA binding constant calculations from Benesi-Hildebrand plot.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chemosphere.2022.134128.
